column5: The Great Migrations of the 1980s
column6: Case of a Small Open Economy
column10: The paper analyzes the impact of migration on a small open economy with a welfare state. It shows that migration can be a Pareto-improving phenomenon, in the sense that it can make all members of the native-born population better off, even if it puts a strain on the welfare state.
column11: Migration can benefit native-born workers; impacts human capital and welfare systems.

column5: Nonparametric Estimation of Dynamic Hedonic Price Models
column6: And the Construction of Residential Housing Price Indices
column10: This paper explores the use of a nonparametric estimation technique, locally weighted regression, to estimate dynamic hedonic price models for residential housing. The technique is used to construct house price indices for two California municipalities.
column11: Nonparametric models outperform standard linear hedonic models for price indices.

column5: Congestible facility rivalry in vertical structures
column10: This paper studies the effects of facility rivalry on capacity and pricing when facilities are congestible and are part of a vertical structure. We show that when facilities are rivals, access fees are lower, but capacity may be either higher or lower than under a monopoly.
column11: Rivalry generally lowers fees; dominant users internalize congestion costs.

column5: Housing Supply and Housing Bubbles
column7: Edward L. Glaeser, Joseph Gyourko & Albert Saiz
column10: This paper argues that the impact of a housing demand shock on house prices and construction depends on the elasticity of housing supply. In areas where housing supply is elastic, demand shocks lead to more construction and less price volatility.
column11: Inelastic supply leads to higher price volatility and larger housing bubbles.

column5: Valuation Standards and Estimation Accuracy
column6: In the Appraisal of a Building Housing Vertical Farming
column10: Modern agriculture is witnessing a transition toward technological and intensive solutions like vertical farming (VF)... This study aims to evaluate the accuracy of the direct capitalization method in the appraisal of a building housing a VF system, comparing it with the international valuation standards (IVS).
column11: Accuracy in ag-tech depends on specialized cap rate determination for high risk.

column5: Cap Rates as a Function of Real Economic Growth
column10: This paper provides a new framework for understanding the relationship between capitalization rates and real economic growth. Using a long-term dataset of U.S. commercial real estate, we show that real economic growth is a primary driver of cap rate compression and expansion.
column11: Real economic growth is a primary driver of long-term cap rate movements.

column5: Determination of the capitalization rate
column6: Based on generalization of the trading segment market information
column7: O. Pirogova, V. Plotnikov & N. Tsekhomsky
column10: This article proposes a method for determining the capitalization rate based on the generalization of market information in the trading segment. The proposed method allows for a more accurate determination of the capitalization rate and, as a result, a more accurate determination of the market value of real estate.
column11: Effective price formation depends on standardizing market transaction data for cap rates.

column5: The Capitalization Rate of Real Estate Property
column6: In Sleman District, Yogyakarta Province, Indonesia
column10: This study aims to determine the capitalization rate of real estate property in Sleman District, Yogyakarta Province, Indonesia. The research used the direct capitalization method to calculate the capitalization rate for different types of real estate property.
column11: Location and property type significantly dictate the cap rate and income potential.

column5: Analytical Implications of Mortgage Lending Value and Bottom Value
column7: F. Salvo, M. De Ruggiero, et al.
column10: This paper explores the analytical implications of MLV and its relationship with the concept of "Bottom Value," which represents the value that a property is likely to maintain even in adverse market conditions.
column11: MLV acts as a stabilized value to filter out bubbles for safer bank lending.

column5: Estimating Market Values from Appraised Values...
column10: This paper presents a simple method for estimating market values from appraised values, based on a model of the appraisal process that does not assume market efficiency. The method is used to estimate market values for the NCREIF index.
column11: Appraisals exhibit smoothing and lag effects compared to actual market transactions.

column5: Mortgage lending valuation bias...
column10: We study the impact of loan-to-value (LTV) regulations on mortgage lending valuation bias. Using data from a large Norwegian bank, we find that appraisers are more likely to provide valuations that are exactly at the LTV threshold when price changes are low or negative.
column11: LTV regulations can incentivize appraisers to provide biased valuations at regulatory thresholds.

column11: AVM outputs influence LTV and ECL; governance must manage collateral uncertainty.

column5: AI governance: a systematic literature review
column7: Batool, A., Zowghi, D. & Bano, M.
column10: As artificial intelligence (AI) transforms a wide range of sectors and drives innovation, it also introduces different types of incidents, illustrating the significant challenges associated with AI deployment... This study presents a systematic literature review that provides the state of the art on AI Governance... we address 4 specific questions: who is governing, what should be governed, when is it being governed, and how is AI being governed.
column11: Systematic review focusing on stakeholders, targets, lifecycle stages, and frameworks for AI governance.

column4: Ethics, Governance, and Policies in Artificial Intelligence
column5: Innovating with Confidence: Embedding AI Governance and Fairness in a Financial Services Risk Management Framework
column7: Michelle Seng Ah Lee, Luciano Floridi & Alexander Denev
column10: An increasing number of financial services (FS) companies are adopting solutions driven by artificial intelligence (AI) to gain operational efficiencies, derive strategic insights, and improve customer engagement. However, reliance on complex models introduces risks related to bias, opacity, and regulatory gaps. Using the risk of unfairness as an example, this paper will introduce the overarching governance strategy and control framework to address the practical challenges in mitigating risks AI introduces. With regulatory implications and industry use cases, this framework will enable leaders to innovate with confidence.
column11: Introduces a governance strategy and control framework to mitigate AI-induced risks (bias, opacity, regulatory gaps) in FS.

column5: AI-driven early warning systems for real estate bubbles
column10: The frequent occurrence of real estate bubbles and their detrimental effects on financial stability highlight the necessity of prompt and precise early-warning systems (EWS). Traditional statistical methods like credit-to-GDP gaps, house price-to-income ratios, and linear econometric models usually fall short in predicting crisis events because of their linear nature and dependence on lagging indicators. However, developments in AI and ML provide fresh perspectives on intricate, nonlinear housing market dynamics, enabling policymakers to identify bubbles sooner and with greater precision. With an emphasis on emerging real estate-driven economies like Dubai, this paper develops and evaluates an AI-driven early-warning framework for identifying real estate bubbles. The study shows that when it comes to event prediction and preparation time, AI-based models perform better than traditional early-warning indicators. This is achieved by integrating banking credit, developer financing flows, liquidity indicators, and macroeconomic variables into a supervised learning framework. The results show how machine learning methods, especially interpretable AI tools and ensemble methods, can give central banks and regulators a reliable, real-time monitoring system. The outcome will be greater success for macroprudential policy.
column11: 1. AI and machine learning models significantly outperform traditional linear econometric indicators (like price-to-income ratios) in predicting housing crises. 2. Integrating "developer-provided financing" alongside traditional bank credit is critical for tracking shadow-banking risks in emerging markets. 3. The use of interpretable AI (specifically SHAP) allows regulators to understand the underlying drivers of risk, moving beyond "black box" predictions. 4. Real-time monitoring using ensemble methods provides a superior lead time for macroprudential policy intervention.
